body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: unknown; driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

**Agent supports (yes).** Pi's REPL lets the user type a follow-up message WHILE a turn is in flight and queues it: Enter steers/queues the buffered text, Alt+Enter inserts a newline. The queued text is not dropped ŌĆö it is consumed after the current turn settles. This satisfies the scenario's essence (user sends a follow-up mid-turn; the runtime buffers it without dropping it).

**Irrlicht observes (partial).** The pi adapter is `FilesUnderRoot` over `~/.pi/agent/sessions/**.jsonl`, and the parser (`core/adapters/inbound/agents/pi/parser.go`) only flips the session back to `ready` on a `turn_done` event ŌĆö i.e. a `{"type":"message","message":{"role":"assistant","stopReason":"stop"}}` line. A `user` line is classified as `user_message` and does NOT by itself emit a turn boundary or a ready transition. So a queued user line landing mid-turn cannot inject a workingŌåÆready flicker: the in-flight turn stays `working` until its own `stopReason:stop`.

In the live recording (pi 0.70.0), pi wrote the queued follow-up's `user` line within ~1ms of the first turn's `stopReason:stop` and began the next turn immediately, so the daemon never observed an intervening ready ŌĆö the two back-to-back transcript turns COLLAPSED into a SINGLE contiguous working span (recorded: one readyŌåÆworkingŌåÆready arc on the rollout session whose working span covers both turns, then a single process_exited). This is the canonical scenario observable, the same single-span shape claudecode/codex produce. The verdict is `partial` rather than `yes` because that single-span observability hinges on the sub-ms queueŌåÆconsume timing: pi consumes the queued message as a distinct transcript turn (two `stop` lines, not one folded turn like codex), so if its consumption were delayed past the classifier's debounce window the queued message could instead surface as a separate workingŌåÆready arc. The negative invariant the scenario cares about (no mid-turn ready flicker, no waiting, ends ready) holds regardless. Hence `partial`, confidence 0.7.

## What proves each signal

- "State stays working when the queued message arrives; no ŌåÆready flicker": the working span is contiguous through the queued user line because only `stopReason:stop` flips to ready and the queued `user` line is not a stop; in the recording no ready transition appears between the first working and the cycle's end. Observable.
- "Cycle eventually ends at ready": final `turn_done` on the rollout session ŌåÆ ready; single `process_exited` at REPL teardown. Observable.
- "No turn boundary on queued arrival": the queued message is a `user_message`, which the parser does not treat as a turn boundary; the boundary is each turn's own `stop`. Observable.
caveats: feature-invisible-but-spec-compliant: pi records the queued follow-up as a SEPARATE transcript turn (two stopReason==stop lines, not folded into one turn like codex), but consumes it within ~1ms of the first turn's stop, so the daemon observes a SINGLE contiguous working span rather than two arcs. The single-span observable depends on that sub-ms queueŌåÆconsume timing; a delayed consumption could instead surface a second workingŌåÆready arc ŌĆö the negative invariant (no mid-turn ready flicker, no waiting, ends ready) holds either way.

sources.note: Only stopReason=="stop" maps to turn_done; a user line is user_message and emits no turn boundary, so a queued user line cannot inject a workingŌåÆready flicker.
Source is FilesUnderRoot over ~/.pi/agent/sessions ŌĆö append-only JSONL transcript the daemon tails for state classification.
pi REPL banner documents Enter (steer/queue) vs Alt+Enter (newline); a second send before the first wait_turn types the follow-up while the turn is in flight.
Cross-adapter contrast: codex folds the queued message into one task_complete; pi does not, hence the partial.
